lines.text: Against Me!. 'Sink, Florida, Sink.' 2002. Laura Jane Grace, James Bowman, the whole machine. This one used to kill me in the bar on Todd Drive.
Grace formed this band in '97 in Naples, Florida—the whole world against her, man. That's what the name *meant*. And then they kept evolving, kept getting louder, kept saying things that mattered.
By 2002 they're on Fat Wreck Chords, full electric, no more acoustic demos. This track—it's about watching a place you love go to hell. I get it. I get it every year.
Grace would build Total Treble Studio down in Elkton a few years later. Had to own the place where the songs came from. Respect that. Respect that hard.
The Bouncing Souls. 'Ole.' 1999. From Hopeless Romantic. Greg Attonito, Pete Steinkopf, Bryan Kienlen—all friends from high school in New Jersey.
This is a singalong. This is a we're-all-in-this-together song. They built their whole thing on that—grassroots shows, their own label Chunksaah Records, fans they called 'True Believers.'
Song like 'Ole' doesn't work unless the people listening feel like they *belong*. And they made sure you did. That's the whole point of punk that actually works.
I'm thinking about the woman who called in around 04:50 last year. Just wanted to hear a voice. Didn't say her name. I played 'Ole' for her and she stayed on the line. That song does that.
Hot Water Music. 'A Good Conspiracy.' 1998. Chuck Ragan, Chris Wollard—dual vocals, call and response. The sound of guys who wanted to break but didn't.

These four moved to Gainesville because Jason Black said the scene was alive there. They reformed after almost breaking up in '98—the touring was killing them. Europe tour, tension, everything falling apart.

They broke up. Then they rehearsed. Then the rehearsals went *well*, so they wrote four new songs, did a farewell show, and basically said 'fuck it, we're still here.' That's the Purge energy right there.
The live album from that show—Live at the Hardback—that's what resilience sounds like. When you almost die and then you don't.

tracks.title: Wonderful Christmastime
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. Paul McCartney's 'Wonderful Christmastime.' Cover band out of San Francisco, formed in '95. They take anything—*anything*—and blast it through a punk filter.
Spike Slawson, C.J. Ramone, Joey Cape, Fat Mike—these guys didn't want to release albums. They just wanted to cover songs during off-time. Then they realized: *that's the album*.

The first album was '97. They called it 'Have a Ball.' Old pop hits. McCartney, Beatles, all that stuff. And they made it *mean* something. That's the trick—respect the source, disrespect the pretense.

There's an old story where Fat Mike got so caught up in 'Wild World' he thought he was Fat Stevens, some guitar-toting extremist, and when Spike sang 'All My Lovin'' Fat Mike thought it was 'Allah my Lovin'' and condemned him to death. That's the Gimmes. Stupid and perfect.
The Lawrence Arms. 'Coming Home.' 2002. From Apathy and Exhaustion. Brendan Kelly, Chris McCaughan—they named the band after the apartment complex they got *evicted from* in the middle of the night.
These guys are from Chicago. They wrote about Chicago, played about Chicago, lived and bled Chicago. All their albums—all of them—are about the city.

This song. This one's about trying to get back to something. Anything. You're tired, you've failed, the world's against you, and you just want to come home. It doesn't work that way, but you keep trying.
Kelly once said they write about experiences shaped by alcohol, good and terrible ones. I respect a man who admits what's actually happening. No bullshit. Just the truth and a four-chord progression.
NOFX. 'Leaving Babylon.' 1994. Fat Mike, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin—they've been doing this since '83. Before I was even born, they were tearing things down.

Punk in Drublic came out in '94. This record—this is it. This is the thing. Gold status eventually. They didn't sign to a major label though. Stayed independent. Fat Mike built Fat Wreck Chords for a reason.
The music video for 'Leave It Alone'—Fat Mike said they decided not to send it to MTV. 'We just didn't want to be part of that machine, of that punk wave.' That's the philosophy right there.
NOFX has always been political. Racist middle-class plight, substance abuse, idiot sons of assholes—they named songs after what they saw. Still doing it now. That's why they matter.

Alkaline Trio. 'Bloodhail.' 2003. Good Mourning. Matt Skiba, Dan Andriano—they're from Chicago. They recorded their first albums at Atlas Studios and they came back there to remember what they sounded like when they were young.
Before this album came out, Skiba and Andriano couldn't quit their day jobs. Bike messengers. Record store clerks. They were broke. They were making music anyway because it *mattered*.

Good Mourning hit number one on the indie charts. Suddenly they could afford rent. 'We've Had Enough' started charting. The band you make in poverty doesn't sound the same as the band you make after. Both are real.

Bloodhail. Death, darkness, that falling-through-the-sky feeling. Alkaline Trio traffics in that. Depression, alcoholism, shattered things. It's beautiful because it's true and it *hurts*. That's the band.
We're {{ELAPSED}} in now. The clock reads {{CLOCK}}. Sunrise is in two hours and change. This one's for the people still listening. Still here. Still together.

tickerItems.text: Multiple structure fires reported isthmus-wide. Smoke drifting east from State Street corridor. Visibility degraded to 0.5 miles in downtown core.
Madison-wide total: 172 confirmed ({{ELAPSED}} elapsed). Pre-dawn surge expected next 60–90 minutes.
Lake Mendota shoreline still active. Cold water temp 28°F. SafeWalk GPS routes re-routing west of Tenney Park.
